items.question: I keep oversleeping. I really ___ buy a louder alarm.
explanations: Correct! A personal feeling takes 'must': 'I must buy a louder alarm'.
'Have to' suggests an outside rule; here it's your own feeling.
'Had to' is past; this is present.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.

items.question: At my company, we ___ clock in by nine.
explanations: Never add 'to' after 'must'.
Correct! A workplace rule (from outside) takes 'have to': 'we have to clock in'.
'Must' is possible, but 'have to' fits an external rule better.
'Had to' is past; this is a present rule.

items.question: Yesterday the car broke down, so I ___ take the bus.
explanations: 'Must' has no past form.
'Have to' is present; this is past ('yesterday').
Correct! Past obligation takes 'had to': 'I had to take the bus'.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.

items.question: It's a secret. You ___ tell anyone.
explanations: 'Don't have to tell' means it's optional — but here it's forbidden.
Correct! Prohibition takes 'mustn't': 'You mustn't tell anyone'.
'Must tell' is an obligation to tell.
'Have to tell' is an obligation to tell.

items.question: It's a casual party — you ___ dress up.
explanations: 'Mustn't dress up' would forbid dressing up.
Correct! No necessity takes 'don't have to': 'You don't have to dress up'.
'Must dress up' is an obligation.
'Have to dress up' is an obligation.

items.question: She ___ wear glasses for driving; it's on her licence.
explanations: Correct! An external rule + 'she' gives 'has to': 'She has to wear glasses'.
'Have to' doesn't agree with 'she'.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.
'Haves to' is not a word.

items.question: Which comes from an OUTSIDE rule?
options: You have to be 18 to vote.
explanations: This is a personal feeling.
Correct! A law (from outside) takes 'have to': 'You have to be 18 to vote'.
This is a personal feeling.
This is the speaker's own sense of obligation.

items.question: Correct this: 'Last week I must finish two reports.'
options: Last week I had to finish two reports.
Last week I must to finish two reports.
Last week I have to finish two reports.
explanations: Correct! Past obligation takes 'had to': 'Last week I had to finish two reports'.
'Must to' is never correct.
'Have to' is present; the sentence is about last week.
'Must' can't refer to the past.

items.question: Passengers ___ fasten their seatbelts during take-off.
explanations: 'Don't have to' would make it optional.
Correct! A stated safety rule takes 'must': 'Passengers must fasten their seatbelts'.
'Mustn't fasten' would forbid it.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.

items.question: The ticket is already paid, so you ___ bring any money.
explanations: 'Mustn't bring money' would forbid bringing money.
Correct! No necessity takes 'don't have to': 'You don't have to bring any money'.
'Must bring' is an obligation.
'Have to bring' is an obligation.
